title: The Golden Source Debate
context: You're in a cross-team architecture forum. The Head of Data has proposed building a 'Golden Source' — a single canonical data model for all employee data across HR, payroll, benefits, and talent management. She's prepared a slide deck showing data inconsistencies across systems and argues that a canonical model will eliminate integration headaches. The CTO is on the call and has expressed frustration about duplicate data and conflicting employee records. You're the solution architect representing the platform team.

questions.prompt: The Head of Data presents her slide: "We have employee data in 14 different systems, each with its own schema. When HR updates an employee's department, it takes 3 days to propagate everywhere. We need one golden source of truth — a canonical employee model that every system reads from." The CTO adds: "This is exactly right. I'm tired of every team maintaining their own copy. Let's build it." How should you respond?

options.text: I agree that data inconsistency is a real problem and it's costing us in duplicate effort, integration bugs, and reporting errors. A canonical model is the right solution — let's define the master employee schema with the core attributes that every system needs, establish clear ownership and governance for the model, and have all 14 systems consume from it. We can start by cataloguing the existing schemas across all systems, identifying the superset of attributes, and then working with each domain team to map their local model to the canonical one. It's a significant investment, but it will pay dividends across every integration we build from here forward.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. You've accepted the solution without questioning the problem framing. A canonical model for all employee data across 14 systems is an enormous undertaking that rarely succeeds. The real issue is propagation delay and inconsistency — those have simpler solutions. By agreeing, you've committed the platform team to building and maintaining the hardest possible thing.

options.text: The data inconsistency problem is real, but I want to separate two things: the propagation delay problem and the 'one model' ambition. For propagation, we can fix the 3-day delay with domain events — when HR updates a department, publish an event and subscribed systems update within minutes. For the canonical model question: which specific fields need to be consistent across all 14 systems? I suspect it's a much smaller surface than the full employee record. That keeps the room on ownership, time model, and reporting need instead of assuming every disagreement proves we need a canonical source with one write path. It also gives the business a lighter answer it can defend.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the architect's framing pattern. You've correctly separated the urgent operational problem (3-day propagation delay) from the strategic ambition (canonical model). The propagation fix is simple — domain events via a message broker. The canonical model question is reframed to scope: you're asking which data actually needs global consistency versus which data is legitimately domain-specific. This prevents the team from trying to canonicalize everything when only a subset matters.

options.text: Canonical data models don't work in practice — I've seen this fail at multiple organisations. Each domain has its own bounded context and its own legitimate view of what 'employee' means. Payroll cares about tax codes, bank details, and pay periods. Talent management cares about skills, development plans, and career aspirations. Benefits cares about dependants and enrolment elections. Trying to unify these into one schema creates a model that's either too generic to be useful or too bloated to maintain. We'd spend more time governing the canonical model than we save on integrations. We should respect the domain boundaries and invest in better point-to-point integration instead. It also gives the steering group a more visible programme answer, which is part of why the heavier path can sound reassuring.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. While this is technically sound (bounded contexts are real), you're lecturing rather than solving. The CTO has expressed frustration and the Head of Data has a real problem. Dismissing the approach without acknowledging the pain and offering an alternative makes you look like you're defending the status quo. Lead with empathy for the problem, then reframe the solution.

questions.prompt: The Head of Data responds: "OK, I hear you on the propagation fix. But we still need a canonical model for reporting. Finance needs a single consistent view of headcount, compensation, and org structure for board reports. We can't have five different answers to 'how many employees do we have?'" How should you respond?

options.text: For reporting specifically, you don't need a canonical model across all operational systems — you need a reporting data store that aggregates and reconciles. That's a different architecture: a read-optimised projection built from domain events, not a shared write model that every system must conform to. Finance gets one answer to headcount, but the operational systems keep their own schemas. That keeps the room on ownership, time model, and reporting need instead of assuming every disagreement proves we need a canonical source with one write path. It also gives the business a lighter answer it can defend.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the architect's framing pattern. This is the key architectural insight. Reporting consistency doesn't require operational model consistency. A CQRS-style read projection — built from events published by operational systems — gives Finance their 'single answer' without forcing every operational system to change its data model. You've addressed the real need (consistent reporting) with the simplest viable architecture.

options.text: That's a valid use case and it gives us a good starting point. Let's begin with the reporting canonical model — we define the core entity model for headcount, compensation, org structure, and legal entity, get sign-off from Finance on the definitions, and build the aggregation layer that populates it. If we start small with the reporting use case and prove the model works, we can gradually expand it into the operational systems as teams see the value. Starting with reporting also means we don't have to change any operational system's write model upfront, which reduces the risk significantly.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. You've conceded to the canonical model framing and proposed starting small. But 'start small and expand' with canonical data models is how they become unmanageable. The reporting need is real but it doesn't require a canonical model — it requires a reporting projection. By accepting the framing, you'll end up building and governing a shared schema that will become a bottleneck.

options.text: The 'how many employees do we have' problem is usually a data quality issue at its root, not a data model issue. Different systems report different numbers because they have different definitions of 'active employee,' stale records that haven't been cleaned up, and inconsistent handling of edge cases like contractors, secondees, and pending starters. Building a new aggregation layer on top of dirty source data gives you a single wrong number instead of five wrong numbers. I'd recommend we invest in a data quality initiative — reconcile the source systems, establish clear data ownership per system, and enforce consistent definitions at the point of entry. Once the source data is clean, the reporting problem largely solves itself.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. While data quality is often a factor, you're dismissing a legitimate architectural need. Finance genuinely needs reconciled headcount figures, and 'clean up each system' is neither actionable nor sufficient — different systems legitimately count differently (contractors, pending starters, etc.). You need to offer a concrete architecture that solves the reconciliation problem.

questions.prompt: The CTO is warming to the event-driven approach but asks: "If we go this route, who owns the employee events? Who decides what an 'employee created' event looks like? Don't we still need a canonical event schema?" The Head of Data adds: "This is exactly my point — you still end up needing a shared model." How should you respond?

options.text: They have a point — we do need governance around event definitions, otherwise we'll end up with 14 teams publishing incompatible events and consumers struggling to make sense of them. I'd suggest we establish a lightweight data architecture working group that reviews all new event definitions, maintains a shared schema registry, and ensures consistency in naming conventions, field types, and versioning. It doesn't need to be heavyweight — a weekly review cadence with representatives from each domain team and the authority to approve or request changes to event schemas. That gives us the benefits of domain events with the consistency guarantee the Head of Data is asking for.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. You've walked into the canonical model trap through the back door. A governance committee that reviews all event schemas will become a bottleneck that slows every team. Event schemas should be owned by the publishing domain, not governed centrally. Consumers transform events into their own model — that's the whole point of domain events.

options.text: The publishing domain owns its events. HR defines what 'employee department changed' looks like because HR is the authority on department assignments. Payroll publishes 'pay run completed' because payroll owns that process. Consumers subscribe to the events they care about and transform them into their own model. There's no central schema — each domain publishes its own facts. The reporting projection is where reconciliation happens, not at the event level. That keeps the room on ownership, time model, and reporting need instead of assuming every disagreement proves we need a canonical source with one write path. It also gives the business a lighter answer it can defend. That keeps domain ownership and reporting design separate instead of letting the canonical-model instinct answer both questions at once.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the architect's framing pattern. This is clean domain-driven design. Each domain publishes its own events in its own language. There's no central 'employee event schema' because 'employee' means different things in different contexts. The reporting store is the single place where these facts get reconciled into a consistent view. You've avoided the governance bottleneck while still delivering the CTO's goal of consistency where it matters — in reporting.

options.text: We could use a schema registry to manage event versions and provide a layer of governance without heavy process. Each team publishes their event schemas to the registry, and consumers can discover available events, validate against published schemas, and get notified of breaking changes. Confluent Schema Registry or a lightweight Azure-native equivalent would work. The registry gives us discoverability and contract validation — a producer can't publish an event that breaks its schema without the registry flagging it — and consumers get a clear contract to code against. It's a pragmatic middle ground between no governance and a central committee. It also gives the steering group a more visible programme answer, which is part of why the heavier path can sound reassuring.
options.explanation: A schema registry is a reasonable tool, but you've skipped the more fundamental question of who defines the schemas. If you introduce a registry without establishing domain ownership, you'll get pressure to 'align' schemas across domains — which is the canonical model by another name. Address the ownership question first.

questions.prompt: A senior engineer in the chat asks: "What about the onboarding flow? When a new employee starts, 8 different systems need to be provisioned — AD account, payroll setup, benefits enrollment, equipment request, etc. Currently the HR team manually enters data into each system. Shouldn't we have a single onboarding orchestration that reads from one employee record?" How should you respond?

options.text: Yes, onboarding orchestration is a great use case for the canonical employee model — and it strengthens the Head of Data's original argument. The onboarding workflow needs to provision 8 systems with consistent employee data: the same name, the same start date, the same department, the same role. If each system has its own version of that data, you get mismatches on day one. A canonical employee record that the onboarding workflow reads from and fans out to each downstream system gives us the consistency guarantee we need. It could serve as the first practical application of the golden source.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. You've been pulled back into the canonical model framing. Onboarding orchestration doesn't require a canonical model — it requires a workflow that reacts to an 'employee onboarded' event and fans out provisioning commands to each system. Each system receives only the data it needs in its own format, not the full canonical record.

options.text: That's a workflow problem, not a data model problem. It sits in our 'workflow/orchestration' domain. The solution is: HR creates the employee and publishes an 'employee onboarded' event with the core facts. An onboarding workflow subscribes to that event and triggers provisioning tasks for each system — AD, payroll, benefits, equipment — each receiving only the fields it needs. No shared model required, a well-designed event and a workflow engine.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the architect's framing pattern. You've correctly classified this as a workflow/orchestration problem using the 6-domain framework, not a data model problem. The onboarding flow is a choreography of provisioning tasks triggered by an event. Each downstream system receives a tailored command, not a dump of the full employee record. This is simpler to build, easier to modify, and doesn't require every system to agree on a schema.

options.text: We should map out all 8 systems and understand the specific data each one needs during onboarding — the required fields, the format expectations, the validation rules, and the sequencing constraints. Some systems may have dependencies on others being provisioned first. This is too complex to solve in this meeting without that analysis. I'd suggest we take an action to do a detailed integration mapping exercise, document the current manual process end to end, and then reconvene with concrete data to inform the architecture decision.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. While mapping the systems is useful, you're deferring when you could be framing the solution pattern right now. The room is looking for architectural direction. You have enough information to state that it's a workflow problem, propose the event-driven approach, and then take the detailed mapping as a follow-up action.

questions.prompt: The Head of Data, looking somewhat deflated, says: "So you're saying we don't need a canonical model at all? Then what about data governance? Who ensures that 'department' means the same thing in HR and Finance? We can't just let every team define things independently." How should you respond?

options.text: You're right, we do need some governance around shared terminology. I'd recommend we establish a formal data dictionary that defines the key shared business terms — department, cost centre, legal entity, employment status, headcount definition — with precise definitions, allowed values, and a named owner for each term. We'd pair that with a lightweight change review committee that meets monthly to review proposed changes to shared definitions. Each domain team would be responsible for ensuring their systems comply with the dictionary definitions. It's a process investment, but without it we'll keep running into the same semantic inconsistencies that started this entire conversation.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. A data dictionary and review committee are governance theatre. They create the illusion of alignment without the enforcement mechanism. In practice, teams will define fields that technically match the dictionary but diverge in meaning. You need to acknowledge the real governance need but solve it architecturally, not with process.

options.text: You're raising a real concern and I don't want to dismiss it. There are a small number of genuinely shared concepts — like org hierarchy, cost centres, and legal entity — that do need cross-domain alignment. My proposal is: identify those shared reference data sets, own them in one place, and publish them as reference data events. But the vast majority of employee attributes are domain-specific and should be governed by their owning domain. Full canonical model for 5 shared concepts, domain ownership for everything else.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the architect's framing pattern. This is a mature architectural response. You've acknowledged the valid concern, identified the small set of genuinely shared concepts, and proposed a concrete ownership model for each. The key insight is that only a handful of reference data items (org hierarchy, cost centres, legal entities) truly need cross-domain consistency. Everything else is legitimately domain-specific. You've given the Head of Data a win on the concepts that matter while avoiding the trap of canonicalizing everything.

options.text: Data governance and data architecture are related but separable concerns, and I think they need different ownership. I'm responsible for the technical architecture — how data flows, where it's stored, how systems integrate. The governance process — who defines business terms, who arbitrates disagreements about what 'department' means in different contexts, who enforces compliance with shared definitions — is something the data team is better positioned to own. I'd suggest the Head of Data works with each domain team to align on shared terminology and establish an ongoing governance cadence, and I'll make sure the architecture supports whatever alignment they reach.
options.explanation: The instinct is understandable in the room. You've abdicated responsibility for a problem that has an architectural solution. Data governance isn't purely a process problem — it requires architectural decisions about what's shared and what's domain-specific. By pushing it to the data team, you're leaving a vacuum that will likely be filled by the canonical model you just argued against.
